title: The Dialect I Forgot
text_zh: 去年春节回老家，我发现自己已经听不懂奶奶说的方言了。这个发现来得猝不及防——她问我要不要吃「馓子」，我愣了整整三秒钟才反应过来那是一种油炸面食。更让我震惊的是，我不仅听不懂，连自己曾经说了十八年的方言也几乎完全丧失了。舌头在嘴巴里笨拙地转动，发出的音调不伦不类，既不是标准的普通话，也不再是地道的方言，像是一个人同时在说两种语言却哪种都说不好。奶奶看着我的样子笑了，但那个笑容里有一种我无法忽视的失落。她说村里的年轻人都不说方言了，连三岁的小孩子都开口就是普通话。「电视里说的，学校里教的，都是那个话，」她说，「我们这个话，再过二十年就没人说了。」那天晚上我躺在奶奶家的老床上，听着窗外的虫鸣——那些虫子用的还是几千年来同样的语言，而我们的方言却在一两代人之间就断裂了。我忽然意识到，方言不只是一种交流工具，它是一种思维方式、一套情感编码、一个理解世界的独特坐标系。有些情感只有用方言才能准确表达——普通话里的「想念」太文雅了，老家方言里那个粗粝的、带着鼻音的「想」字，才真正装得下一个人离乡二十年的全部重量。当我丧失了方言，我失去的不只是一种语言能力，而是与故乡之间一条无形的脐带。
text_pinyin: Qùnián Chūnjié huí lǎojiā, wǒ fāxiàn zìjǐ yǐjīng tīng bù dǒng nǎinai shuō de fāngyán le. Zhège fāxiàn lái de cùbùjífáng——tā wèn wǒ yào bú yào chī「sǎnzi」, wǒ lèng le zhěngzhěng sān miǎozhōng cái fǎnyìng guòlái nà shì yī zhǒng yóuzhá miànshí. Gèng ràng wǒ zhènjīng de shì, wǒ bùjǐn tīng bù dǒng, lián zìjǐ céngjīng shuō le shíbā nián de fāngyán yě jīhū wánquán sàngshī le. Shétou zài zuǐba lǐ bènzhuō de zhuǎndòng, fāchū de yīndiào bùlúnbùlèi, jì bú shì biāozhǔn de pǔtōnghuà, yě bú zài shì dìdao de fāngyán, xiàng shì yī gè rén tóngshí zài shuō liǎng zhǒng yǔyán què nǎ zhǒng dōu shuō bù hǎo. Nǎinai kàn zhe wǒ de yàngzi xiào le, dàn nàge xiàoróng lǐ yǒu yī zhǒng wǒ wúfǎ hūshì de shīluò. Tā shuō cūn lǐ de niánqīngrén dōu bù shuō fāngyán le, lián sān suì de xiǎo háizi dōu kāikǒu jiù shì pǔtōnghuà. 「Diànshì lǐ shuō de, xuéxiào lǐ jiào de, dōu shì nàge huà,」tā shuō, 「wǒmen zhège huà, zài guò èrshí nián jiù méi rén shuō le.」Nà tiān wǎnshàng wǒ tǎng zài nǎinai jiā de lǎo chuáng shàng, tīng zhe chuāng wài de chóng míng——nàxiē chóngzi yòng de háishi jǐ qiān nián lái tóngyàng de yǔyán, ér wǒmen de fāngyán què zài yī liǎng dài rén zhījiān jiù duànliè le. Wǒ hūrán yìshí dào, fāngyán bù zhǐ shì yī zhǒng jiāoliú gōngjù, tā shì yī zhǒng sīwéi fāngshì, yī tào qínggǎn biānmǎ, yī gè lǐjiě shìjiè de dútè zuòbiāo xì. Yǒuxiē qínggǎn zhǐ yǒu yòng fāngyán cái néng zhǔnquè biǎodá——pǔtōnghuà lǐ de「xiǎngniàn」tài wényǎ le, lǎojiā fāngyán lǐ nàge cūlì de, dài zhe bíyīn de「xiǎng」zì, cái zhēnzhèng zhuāng de xià yī gè rén lí xiāng èrshí nián de quánbù zhòngliàng. Dāng wǒ sàngshī le fāngyán, wǒ shīqù de bù zhǐ shì yī zhǒng yǔyán nénglì, ér shì yǔ gùxiāng zhījiān yī tiáo wúxíng de qídài.
text_en: When I went home for Spring Festival last year, I discovered I could no longer understand the dialect my grandmother spoke. The realization hit without warning—she asked if I wanted some 'sanzi,' and I froze for a full three seconds before realizing she meant a type of deep-fried pastry. Even more shocking, I couldn't just not understand; the dialect I had spoken for eighteen years had been almost completely lost. My tongue turned clumsily in my mouth, producing tones that were neither fish nor fowl—not standard Mandarin, no longer authentic dialect, like a person trying to speak two languages simultaneously and managing neither. Grandmother smiled at the sight of me, but in that smile was a sadness I couldn't ignore. She said the young people in the village all stopped speaking dialect; even three-year-olds open their mouths in Mandarin. 'What they hear on TV, what they learn at school—it's all that language,' she said. 'Our language—in another twenty years no one will speak it.' That night I lay on the old bed in grandmother's house, listening to the insects outside the window—those insects still use the same language they've used for thousands of years, yet our dialect fractured within a generation or two. I suddenly realized that dialect is not just a communication tool; it is a way of thinking, a system of emotional encoding, a unique coordinate system for understanding the world. Some emotions can only be accurately expressed in dialect—the Mandarin word for 'missing someone' is too refined; only the rough, nasal-toned word for 'missing' in our hometown dialect can truly hold the full weight of a person's twenty years away from home. When I lost my dialect, what I lost was not merely a linguistic ability but an invisible umbilical cord connecting me to my hometown.

title: The Handwriting That Stayed Behind
text_zh: 整理父亲遗物的时候，我在他的书房里找到了一个装满信件的铁盒子。信件按年份排列，从一九八二年到二零零五年，之后就再也没有了——那是他和母亲最后一次通过纸质信件沟通的年份，之后所有的交流都转移到了手机短信和后来的微信上。我拿起最早的那封信，纸张已经泛黄变脆，但父亲的钢笔字迹依然清晰——那是一种带着明显时代特征的字体，端正、克制、字距均匀，每一笔都有着严格的起承转合，像是在写毛笔字的人被迫改用了钢笔。信的内容很简单，是他刚参加工作时写给母亲的第一封信，汇报新单位的情况。但字里行间的紧张和生涩是藏不住的——他用了三段来描述食堂的伙食，用了半页纸来解释宿舍的床位分配，唯独只用了最后一句话来表达想念：「盼回信。」我又翻开二零零五年的最后一封信，父亲的字迹明显变了——变得放松、潦草，笔画之间少了年轻时的拘谨，多了一种中年人特有的随意和坦然。信的结尾是：「冰箱里有排骨汤，回来热一下就能喝。」这句话没有任何文学性，但我读到的时候哭了。从「盼回信」到「冰箱里有排骨汤」，二十三年的光阴浓缩在笔迹的变化里。一个人的笔迹就是他一生的心电图——年轻时紧绷、中年时舒展、晚年时颤抖。当我们不再手写，消失的不只是一种书写方式，而是人生这条曲线上最真实的波纹。
text_pinyin: Zhěnglǐ fùqīn yíwù de shíhou, wǒ zài tā de shūfáng lǐ zhǎo dào le yī gè zhuāng mǎn xìnjiàn de tiě hézi. Xìnjiàn àn niánfèn páiliè, cóng yī jiǔ bā èr nián dào èr líng líng wǔ nián, zhīhòu jiù zài yě méiyǒu le——nà shì tā hé mǔqīn zuìhòu yī cì tōngguò zhǐzhì xìnjiàn gōutōng de niánfèn, zhīhòu suǒyǒu de jiāoliú dōu zhuǎnyí dào le shǒujī duǎnxìn hé hòulái de Wēixìn shàng. Wǒ ná qǐ zuìzǎo de nà fēng xìn, zhǐzhāng yǐjīng fànhuáng biàn cuì, dàn fùqīn de gāngbǐ zìjì yīrán qīngxī——nà shì yī zhǒng dài zhe míngxiǎn shídài tèzhēng de zìtǐ, duānzhèng, kèzhì, zìjù jūnyún, měi yī bǐ dōu yǒu zhe yángé de qǐchéngzhuǎnhé, xiàng shì zài xiě máobǐ zì de rén bèi pò gǎi yòng le gāngbǐ. Xìn de nèiróng hěn jiǎndān, shì tā gāng cānjiā gōngzuò shí xiě gěi mǔqīn de dì yī fēng xìn, huìbào xīn dānwèi de qíngkuàng. Dàn zì lǐ háng jiān de jǐnzhāng hé shēngsè shì cáng bú zhù de——tā yòng le sān duàn lái miáoshù shítáng de huǒshí, yòng le bàn yè zhǐ lái jiěshì sùshè de chuángwèi fēnpèi, wéidú zhǐ yòng le zuìhòu yī jù huà lái biǎodá xiǎngniàn: 「Pàn huí xìn.」Wǒ yòu fān kāi èr líng líng wǔ nián de zuìhòu yī fēng xìn, fùqīn de zìjì míngxiǎn biàn le——biàn de fàngsōng, liáocǎo, bǐhuà zhījiān shǎo le niánqīng shí de jūjǐn, duō le yī zhǒng zhōngnián rén tèyǒu de suíyì hé tǎnrán. Xìn de jiéwěi shì: 「Bīngxiāng lǐ yǒu páigǔ tāng, huílái rè yīxià jiù néng hē.」Zhè jù huà méiyǒu rènhé wénxuéxìng, dàn wǒ dú dào de shíhou kū le. Cóng「pàn huí xìn」dào「bīngxiāng lǐ yǒu páigǔ tāng」, èrshísān nián de guāngyīn nóngsuō zài bǐjì de biànhuà lǐ. Yī gè rén de bǐjì jiù shì tā yīshēng de xīndiàntú——niánqīng shí jǐnbēng, zhōngnián shí shūzhǎn, wǎnnián shí chàndǒu. Dāng wǒmen bú zài shǒuxiě, xiāoshī de bù zhǐ shì yī zhǒng shūxiě fāngshì, ér shì rénshēng zhè tiáo qūxiàn shàng zuì zhēnshí de bōwén.
text_en: While sorting through my father's belongings, I found a tin box full of letters in his study. They were arranged by year, from 1982 to 2005, and after that there were no more—that was the last year he and my mother communicated through paper letters, after which all correspondence shifted to text messages and eventually WeChat. I picked up the earliest letter; the paper had yellowed and turned brittle, but my father's fountain-pen handwriting was still clear—a script bearing obvious marks of its era, upright, restrained, evenly spaced, every stroke following strict conventions of beginning, transition, and conclusion, like someone trained in brush calligraphy forced to switch to a fountain pen. The content was simple: the first letter he wrote to my mother upon starting his new job, reporting on conditions at his new work unit. But the nervousness and rawness between the lines couldn't be hidden—he used three paragraphs to describe the cafeteria food, half a page to explain the dormitory bunk assignment, and devoted only the final sentence to expressing how much he missed her: 'Awaiting your reply.' I then turned to the last letter from 2005. My father's handwriting had clearly changed—it had become relaxed, hasty, losing the young man's stiffness and gaining a middle-aged man's particular ease and frankness. The letter's closing read: 'There's pork-rib soup in the fridge—just heat it up when you get home.' That sentence has no literary quality whatsoever, but I cried when I read it. From 'awaiting your reply' to 'there's pork-rib soup in the fridge,' twenty-three years of time are condensed in the changes of handwriting. A person's handwriting is the electrocardiogram of their life—taut in youth, expansive in middle age, trembling in old age. When we stop writing by hand, what vanishes is not just a mode of writing but the most authentic ripples on the curve of a life.

title: The Passport of Scars
text_zh: 我的左手腕上有一道两厘米长的疤痕，那是七岁时从树上摔下来留下的。右膝盖上有一片不规则的白色疤痕，是十二岁骑自行车摔倒被砂石路面擦伤的结果。左脚大脚趾的指甲永远长不平整，因为十五岁踢球时被人狠狠踩了一脚。这些伤疤就像一本私人的护照，每一道都盖着一个特定年龄和场景的印章。有一次体检的时候，医生看到我身上大大小小的疤痕，好奇地问我是不是从事什么高危职业。我说不是，这些都是成长的正常损耗——每一道伤疤背后都有一个关于冒险、冲动或者纯粹倒霉的故事。我注意到一个有趣的现象：当我跟别人展示伤疤的时候，对方几乎总会回应一个自己的伤疤故事。这似乎是一种人际交往中的对称礼仪——你展示了一个脆弱的部分，对方也会回馈一个同等程度的脆弱。伤疤在某种意义上是最诚实的社交货币，因为它不能伪造。每一道伤疤都是一个你确实经历过某件事的不可否认的证据。随着年龄增长，新的伤疤越来越少了——不是因为世界变得更安全了，而是因为我们变得更谨慎了。我有时候会怀念那个不知天高地厚、动不动就摔得遍体鳞伤的年纪。那些伤疤提醒我，我曾经是一个不怕疼的人。而现在，我最害怕的伤痕，是那些留在心里、长在皮肤底下、永远无法展示给别人看的那一种。
text_pinyin: Wǒ de zuǒ shǒu wàn shàng yǒu yī dào liǎng límǐ cháng de bāhén, nà shì qī suì shí cóng shù shàng shuāi xiàlái liú xià de. Yòu xīgài shàng yǒu yī piàn bù guīzé de báisè bāhén, shì shí'èr suì qí zìxíngchē shuāidǎo bèi shāshí lùmiàn cā shāng de jiéguǒ. Zuǒ jiǎo dà jiǎozhǐ de zhǐjia yǒngyuǎn zhǎng bù píngzhěng, yīnwèi shíwǔ suì tī qiú shí bèi rén hěnhěn cǎi le yī jiǎo. Zhèxiē shāngbā jiù xiàng yī běn sīrén de hùzhào, měi yī dào dōu gài zhe yī gè tèdìng niánlíng hé chǎngjǐng de yìnzhāng. Yǒu yī cì tǐjiǎn de shíhou, yīshēng kàn dào wǒ shēnshàng dàdàxiǎoxiǎo de bāhén, hàoqí de wèn wǒ shì bú shì cóngshì shénme gāowēi zhíyè. Wǒ shuō bú shì, zhèxiē dōu shì chéngzhǎng de zhèngcháng sǔnhào——měi yī dào shāngbā bèihòu dōu yǒu yī gè guānyú màoxiǎn, chōngdòng huòzhě chúncuì dǎoméi de gùshi. Wǒ zhùyì dào yī gè yǒuqù de xiànxiàng: dāng wǒ gēn biérén zhǎnshì shāngbā de shíhou, duìfāng jīhū zǒng huì huíyìng yī gè zìjǐ de shāngbā gùshi. Zhè sìhū shì yī zhǒng rénjì jiāowǎng zhōng de duìchèn lǐyí——nǐ zhǎnshì le yī gè cuìruò de bùfèn, duìfāng yě huì huìkuì yī gè tóngděng chéngdù de cuìruò. Shāngbā zài mǒu zhǒng yìyì shàng shì zuì chéngshí de shèjiāo huòbì, yīnwèi tā bù néng wěizào. Měi yī dào shāngbā dōu shì yī gè nǐ quèshí jīnglì guò mǒu jiàn shì de bùkě fǒurèn de zhèngjù. Suízhe niánlíng zēngzhǎng, xīn de shāngbā yuèláiyuè shǎo le——bú shì yīnwèi shìjiè biàn de gèng ānquán le, ér shì yīnwèi wǒmen biàn de gèng jǐnshèn le. Wǒ yǒu shíhou huì huáiniàn nàge bù zhī tiān gāo dì hòu, dòng bú dòng jiù shuāi de biàntǐ línshāng de niánjì. Nàxiē shāngbā tíxǐng wǒ, wǒ céngjīng shì yī gè bú pà téng de rén. Ér xiànzài, wǒ zuì hàipà de shānghén, shì nàxiē liú zài xīnlǐ, zhǎng zài pífū dǐxia, yǒngyuǎn wúfǎ zhǎnshì gěi biérén kàn de nà yī zhǒng.
text_en: On my left wrist there's a two-centimeter scar, left from falling out of a tree when I was seven. On my right knee there's an irregular patch of white scarring—the result of crashing my bicycle on a gravel road at twelve. The nail on my left big toe will never grow evenly, because at fifteen someone stamped hard on my foot during a soccer match. These scars are like a private passport, each one stamped with a particular age and scene. During a medical checkup once, the doctor noticed all the scars large and small on my body and asked with curiosity whether I worked in some high-risk profession. I said no—these are the normal wear and tear of growing up; behind every scar is a story about risk-taking, impulse, or sheer bad luck. I've noticed an interesting phenomenon: when I show someone a scar, the other person almost always responds with a scar story of their own. This seems to be a kind of symmetrical etiquette in interpersonal exchange—you reveal a vulnerable part of yourself, and the other person reciprocates with a vulnerability of equal degree. Scars are, in a sense, the most honest social currency, because they cannot be forged. Every scar is undeniable proof that you actually experienced something. As I've grown older, new scars have become fewer—not because the world has grown safer, but because we've grown more cautious. Sometimes I miss that age when I had no idea of my own limits and was forever tumbling into bruises. Those scars remind me that I was once someone who wasn't afraid of pain. And now, the scars I fear most are the kind that stay inside the heart, grow beneath the skin, and can never be shown to anyone.

title: The Name I Was Almost Given
text_zh: 前几年整理老家的旧物时，我在母亲的日记里发现了一个令我震惊的细节——我出生前，父母给我准备了三个候选名字，而我最终获得的这个名字其实排在第三位。第一候选名字叫「致远」，取自诸葛亮的「非淡泊无以明志，非宁静无以致远」；第二候选名字叫「若水」，取自老子的「上善若水」。最终定下现在这个名字的原因竟然异常朴素——我出生那天爷爷正好在看一本关于植物的书，看到了某一种树的名字，觉得好听，就一锤定音了。我有时候会想，如果我叫「致远」或者「若水」，我的人生会不会有所不同？这个想法当然很荒谬——名字不会改变一个人的命运。但名字确实会影响一个人对自己的想象。一个叫「致远」的人可能会不自觉地在人生选择中寻找宏大叙事的可能，而一个叫「若水」的人可能更容易被柔和的力量所吸引。名字是父母给孩子的第一份礼物，也是第一个期望。它在孩子还没有意识到自我之前，就已经开始悄悄地塑造着一个关于「我应该成为什么样的人」的潜在叙事。当然，大多数人最终都会偏离父母的命名期望，走出一条完全属于自己的路。但那个名字，像一枚嵌在出发点的坐标钉，即使你走得再远，回头看的时候，它还是标记着你最初被期许的方向。而那些你差点拥有的名字，则像是你差点拥有的平行人生——它们安静地存在于某个抽屉的某本日记里，等待着被偶然翻到的那一天。
text_pinyin: Qián jǐ nián zhěnglǐ lǎojiā de jiùwù shí, wǒ zài mǔqīn de rìjì lǐ fāxiàn le yī gè lìng wǒ zhènjīng de xìjié——wǒ chūshēng qián, fùmǔ gěi wǒ zhǔnbèi le sān gè hòuxuǎn míngzi, ér wǒ zuìzhōng huòdé de zhège míngzi qíshí pái zài dì sān wèi. Dì yī hòuxuǎn míngzi jiào「Zhìyuǎn」, qǔ zì Zhūgě Liàng de「fēi dànbó wúyǐ míngzhì, fēi níngjìng wúyǐ zhìyuǎn」; dì èr hòuxuǎn míngzi jiào「Ruòshuǐ」, qǔ zì Lǎozǐ de「shàng shàn ruò shuǐ」. Zuìzhōng dìng xià xiànzài zhège míngzi de yuányīn jìngrán yìcháng púsù——wǒ chūshēng nà tiān yéye zhènghǎo zài kàn yī běn guānyú zhíwù de shū, kàn dào le mǒu yī zhǒng shù de míngzi, juéde hǎotīng, jiù yīchuí dìngyīn le. Wǒ yǒu shíhou huì xiǎng, rúguǒ wǒ jiào「Zhìyuǎn」huò「Ruòshuǐ」, wǒ de rénshēng huì bú huì yǒusuǒ bùtóng? Zhège xiǎngfǎ dāngrán hěn huāngmiù——míngzi bú huì gǎibiàn yī gè rén de mìngyùn. Dàn míngzi quèshí huì yǐngxiǎng yī gè rén duì zìjǐ de xiǎngxiàng. Yī gè jiào「Zhìyuǎn」de rén kěnéng huì bù zìjué de zài rénshēng xuǎnzé zhōng xúnzhǎo hóngdà xùshì de kěnéng, ér yī gè jiào「Ruòshuǐ」de rén kěnéng gèng róngyì bèi róuhé de lìliàng suǒ xīyǐn. Míngzi shì fùmǔ gěi háizi de dì yī fèn lǐwù, yě shì dì yī gè qīwàng. Tā zài háizi hái méiyǒu yìshí dào zìwǒ zhīqián, jiù yǐjīng kāishǐ qiāoqiāo de sùzào zhe yī gè guānyú「wǒ yīnggāi chéngwéi shénme yàng de rén」de qiánzài xùshì. Dāngrán, dàduōshù rén zuìzhōng dōu huì piānlí fùmǔ de mìngmíng qīwàng, zǒuchū yī tiáo wánquán shǔyú zìjǐ de lù. Dàn nàge míngzi, xiàng yī méi qiàn zài chūfā diǎn de zuòbiāo dīng, jíshǐ nǐ zǒu de zài yuǎn, huítóu kàn de shíhou, tā háishi biāojì zhe nǐ zuìchū bèi qīxǔ de fāngxiàng. Ér nàxiē nǐ chàdiǎn yōngyǒu de míngzi, zé xiàng shì nǐ chàdiǎn yōngyǒu de píngxíng rénshēng——tāmen ānjìng de cúnzài yú mǒu gè chōuti de mǒu běn rìjì lǐ, děngdài zhe bèi ǒurán fān dào de nà yī tiān.
text_en: A few years ago while sorting through old belongings at my family home, I discovered a startling detail in my mother's diary—before I was born, my parents had prepared three candidate names for me, and the name I ended up with was actually ranked third. The first candidate was 'Zhiyuan,' drawn from Zhuge Liang's maxim 'Without tranquility one cannot see far'; the second was 'Ruoshui,' from Laozi's 'Supreme goodness is like water.' The reason the current name was ultimately chosen turned out to be remarkably prosaic—on the day I was born, my grandfather happened to be reading a book about plants, saw the name of a certain tree, thought it sounded nice, and that settled the matter. I sometimes wonder: if I had been called 'Zhiyuan' or 'Ruoshui,' would my life have been any different? The thought is of course absurd—a name doesn't change a person's destiny. But a name does influence a person's imagination of themselves. Someone named 'Zhiyuan' might unconsciously seek grand-narrative possibilities in their life choices, while someone named 'Ruoshui' might more easily be drawn to gentle forces. A name is the first gift parents give a child, and the first expectation. Before the child has even developed a sense of self, it has already begun quietly shaping a latent narrative about 'what kind of person I should become.' Of course, most people ultimately deviate from their parents' naming expectations and forge a path entirely their own. But that name, like a coordinate pin embedded at the starting point, no matter how far you travel, when you look back it still marks the direction in which you were first hoped to go. And those names you were almost given are like the parallel lives you almost lived—they exist quietly in some diary in some drawer, waiting for the day they are happened upon.

title: The Accent That Won't Leave
text_zh: 在北京生活了十五年之后，我以为自己的普通话已经完美无瑕了。直到有一天，一个出租车司机听了我说的三句话就准确地猜出了我的老家。「你是湖北人吧？」他说，「n和l你分不清，而且你的四声在句尾总是会不自觉地往上挑一下。」我当时又惊讶又窘迫——那些我以为已经被十五年的北方生活磨平的语音特征，原来一直藏在我说话的底层代码里，就像电脑系统深处那些无法被完全删除的预装软件。后来我开始留意自己的发音，发现那个司机说得完全正确。尤其是在情绪激动的时候——吵架、着急、或者特别开心的时候——方言的底色会像海水退潮后露出的礁石一样浮现出来。有一次跟朋友打电话，说到激动处忍不住蹦出了一句纯正的家乡话，挂了电话之后自己愣了好几秒。那一刻我意识到，口音不是一种语言习惯，而是一种身份标记——它比身份证更顽固，比户口簿更诚实。你可以更改籍贯、更换城市、甚至更改国籍，但你无法完全更改你的口音。它是你的出生地通过声带刻在你身上的水印，是你在这个世界上最早被格式化的那一层底色。有些语言学家说，一个人在七岁之前接触的语音环境会永久性地塑造他的发音器官。如果这是真的，那我们每个人的喉咙里都住着一个七岁的自己，在我们以为早就长大了的时候，悄悄地替我们发出那些暴露来历的声音。
text_pinyin: Zài Běijīng shēnghuó le shíwǔ nián zhīhòu, wǒ yǐwéi zìjǐ de pǔtōnghuà yǐjīng wánměi wúxiá le. Zhídào yǒu yī tiān, yī gè chūzūchē sījī tīng le wǒ shuō de sān jù huà jiù zhǔnquè de cāi chū le wǒ de lǎojiā. 「Nǐ shì Húběi rén ba?」tā shuō, 「n hé l nǐ fēn bù qīng, érqiě nǐ de sì shēng zài jù wěi zǒng shì huì bù zìjué de wǎng shàng tiāo yīxià.」Wǒ dāngshí yòu jīngyà yòu jiǒngpò——nàxiē wǒ yǐwéi yǐjīng bèi shíwǔ nián de běifāng shēnghuó mó píng de yǔyīn tèzhēng, yuánlái yīzhí cáng zài wǒ shuōhuà de dǐcéng dàimǎ lǐ, jiù xiàng diànnǎo xìtǒng shēnchù nàxiē wúfǎ bèi wánquán shānchú de yùzhuāng ruǎnjiàn. Hòulái wǒ kāishǐ liúyì zìjǐ de fāyīn, fāxiàn nàge sījī shuō de wánquán zhèngquè. Yóuqí shì zài qíngxù jīdòng de shíhou——chǎojià, zháojí, huòzhě tèbié kāixīn de shíhou——fāngyán de dǐsè huì xiàng hǎishuǐ tuìcháo hòu lòuchū de jiāoshí yīyàng fúxiàn chūlái. Yǒu yī cì gēn péngyǒu dǎ diànhuà, shuō dào jīdòng chù rěn bú zhù bèng chū le yī jù chúnzhèng de jiāxiāng huà, guà le diànhuà zhīhòu zìjǐ lèng le hǎo jǐ miǎo. Nà yī kè wǒ yìshí dào, kǒuyīn bú shì yī zhǒng yǔyán xíguàn, ér shì yī zhǒng shēnfèn biāojì——tā bǐ shēnfènzhèng gèng wángù, bǐ hùkǒubù gèng chéngshí. Nǐ kěyǐ gēnggǎi jíguàn, gēnghuàn chéngshì, shènzhì gēnggǎi guójí, dàn nǐ wúfǎ wánquán gēnggǎi nǐ de kǒuyīn. Tā shì nǐ de chūshēngdì tōngguò shēngdài kè zài nǐ shēnshàng de shuǐyìn, shì nǐ zài zhège shìjiè shàng zuìzǎo bèi géshìhuà de nà yī céng dǐsè. Yǒuxiē yǔyánxuéjiā shuō, yī gè rén zài qī suì zhīqián jiēchù de yǔyīn huánjìng huì yǒngjiǔxìng de sùzào tā de fāyīn qìguān. Rúguǒ zhè shì zhēn de, nà wǒmen měi gè rén de hóulóng lǐ dōu zhù zhe yī gè qī suì de zìjǐ, zài wǒmen yǐwéi zǎo jiù zhǎngdà le de shíhou, qiāoqiāo de tì wǒmen fāchū nàxiē bàolù láilì de shēngyīn.
text_en: After fifteen years of living in Beijing, I thought my Mandarin was flawless. Until one day a taxi driver heard me say three sentences and accurately guessed my hometown. 'You're from Hubei, right?' he said. 'You can't distinguish n from l, and your fourth tone always lifts unconsciously at the end of sentences.' I was both astonished and embarrassed—those phonetic features I thought fifteen years of northern living had smoothed away had actually been hiding in the base code of my speech all along, like pre-installed software deep in a computer system that can never be fully deleted. Later I began paying attention to my own pronunciation and found the driver was completely right. Especially when emotions ran high—during arguments, in a rush, or when particularly happy—the substrate of dialect would surface like reef exposed by the retreating tide. Once, while talking to a friend on the phone, at an excited moment I couldn't help blurting out a sentence in pure hometown speech; after hanging up I sat stunned for several seconds. In that moment I realized that an accent is not a linguistic habit but an identity marker—more stubborn than an ID card, more honest than a household register. You can change your registered birthplace, switch cities, even change your nationality, but you cannot fully change your accent. It is a watermark your birthplace etched onto you through your vocal cords, the earliest layer of formatting applied to you in this world. Some linguists say that the phonetic environment a person encounters before age seven permanently shapes their articulatory organs. If that's true, then inside every one of our throats lives a seven-year-old self, quietly issuing the sounds that give away our origins at the very moments we think we've long since grown up.

title: The Mirror at the Barbershop
text_zh: 我去同一家理发店理了十二年的头发。理发师老陈是个话不多的四十多岁的男人，他从不问我「想剪什么样的」这类问题，因为十二年来我每次剪的发型都一模一样——短、利落、不需要打理。但老陈跟我说过一件让我很难忘的事。他说理发店的镜子是世界上最残酷的镜子，因为它会让你在两个月内看到自己从「刚理完发的精神面貌」退化到「需要理发的疲惫面貌」的全过程。但比这更残酷的是长期的变化——他看着老主顾们一年年地回来，头发从黑变灰、从灰变白、从厚变薄，甚至到最后彻底消失。「我给一个老先生理了二十年的头发，」他说，「最开始是整头乌黑浓密的头发，到后来只剩下后脑勺一圈白色的绒毛。最后一次他来的时候，我其实没什么可剪的了，但他还是坐在椅子上，让我用推子慢慢地修了二十分钟。走的时候他看着镜子里的自己，说了一句：'老陈，你是看着我一根一根掉头发的人。'」老陈说那句话让他沉默了很久。他后来意识到，理发师和客人之间的关系是一种非常特殊的时间见证——你们不是亲人、不是朋友、不是同事，但你们之间有一面镜子，它忠实地记录了一个人从青年到老年的物理变化。「有时候我觉得我剪的不是头发，」老陈说，「是时间。每一刀下去，剪掉的都是又过去的一段日子。」
text_pinyin: Wǒ qù tóngyī jiā lǐfàdiàn lǐ le shí'èr nián de tóufa. Lǐfàshī Lǎo Chén shì gè huà bù duō de sìshí duō suì de nánrén, tā cóng bù wèn wǒ「xiǎng jiǎn shénme yàng de」zhè lèi wèntí, yīnwèi shí'èr nián lái wǒ měi cì jiǎn de fàxíng dōu yīmóyīyàng——duǎn, lìluò, bù xūyào dǎlǐ. Dàn Lǎo Chén gēn wǒ shuō guò yī jiàn ràng wǒ hěn nánwàng de shì. Tā shuō lǐfàdiàn de jìngzi shì shìjiè shàng zuì cánkù de jìngzi, yīnwèi tā huì ràng nǐ zài liǎng gè yuè nèi kàn dào zìjǐ cóng「gāng lǐ wán fà de jīngshén miànmào」tuìhuà dào「xūyào lǐfà de píbèi miànmào」de quán guòchéng. Dàn bǐ zhè gèng cánkù de shì chángqī de biànhuà——tā kàn zhe lǎo zhǔgùmen yī nián nián de huílái, tóufa cóng hēi biàn huī, cóng huī biàn bái, cóng hòu biàn báo, shènzhì dào zuìhòu chèdǐ xiāoshī. 「Wǒ gěi yī gè lǎo xiānshēng lǐ le èrshí nián de tóufa,」tā shuō, 「zuì kāishǐ shì zhěng tóu wūhēi nóngmì de tóufa, dào hòulái zhǐ shèng xià hòunǎosháo yī quān báisè de róngmáo. Zuìhòu yī cì tā lái de shíhou, wǒ qíshí méi shénme kě jiǎn de le, dàn tā háishi zuò zài yǐzi shàng, ràng wǒ yòng tuīzi mànmàn de xiū le èrshí fēnzhōng. Zǒu de shíhou tā kàn zhe jìngzi lǐ de zìjǐ, shuō le yī jù: 'Lǎo Chén, nǐ shì kàn zhe wǒ yī gēn yī gēn diào tóufa de rén.'」Lǎo Chén shuō nà jù huà ràng tā chénmò le hěn jiǔ. Tā hòulái yìshí dào, lǐfàshī hé kèrén zhījiān de guānxi shì yī zhǒng fēicháng tèshū de shíjiān jiànzhèng——nǐmen bú shì qīnrén, bú shì péngyǒu, bú shì tóngshì, dàn nǐmen zhījiān yǒu yī miàn jìngzi, tā zhōngshí de jìlù le yī gè rén cóng qīngnián dào lǎonián de wùlǐ biànhuà. 「Yǒu shíhou wǒ juéde wǒ jiǎn de bú shì tóufa,」Lǎo Chén shuō, 「shì shíjiān. Měi yī dāo xiàqù, jiǎn diào de dōu shì yòu guòqù de yī duàn rìzi.」
text_en: I've been getting my hair cut at the same barbershop for twelve years. The barber, Old Chen, is a quiet man in his forties who never asks me 'what style would you like?'—because for twelve years I've gotten the exact same cut every time: short, clean, no maintenance needed. But Old Chen once told me something I can't forget. He said the barbershop mirror is the cruelest mirror in the world, because within two months it shows you the entire process of your appearance deteriorating from 'freshly-cut alertness' to 'needs-a-haircut weariness.' But crueler still is the long-term change—he watches his regulars come back year after year, hair going from black to gray, from gray to white, from thick to thin, until finally it vanishes entirely. 'I cut one old gentleman's hair for twenty years,' he said. 'At first it was a full head of jet-black, dense hair. Eventually only a ring of white fuzz remained around the back of his head. The last time he came in, I really had nothing left to cut, but he still sat in the chair and let me trim with the clippers slowly for twenty minutes. As he was leaving he looked at himself in the mirror and said: 「Old Chen, you're the person who watched me lose my hair one strand at a time.「' Old Chen said that remark left him silent for a long time. He later realized that the relationship between a barber and a client is a very special form of time-witnessing—you are not relatives, not friends, not colleagues, but between you there is a mirror that faithfully records one person's physical transformation from youth to old age. 'Sometimes I feel I'm not cutting hair,' Old Chen said, 'but time. Every snip of the scissors cuts away another stretch of days that have passed.'

title: The Archaeology of a Bookshelf
text_zh: 搬家的时候，我不得不面对一个积累了十五年的书架。清理的过程变成了一次自我考古——每一本书的购买时间和阅读状态都精确地对应着我人生的某个阶段。最底层是大学时代的书：萨特的《存在与虚无》、加缪的《局外人》、几本读了一半的哲学入门读物。这些书的书页已经泛黄，空白处写满了我当时自以为很深刻的批注——现在回头看，那些批注充满了一个二十岁年轻人特有的认真和天真。中间几层是二十五到三十岁之间买的书，类型发生了明显的转变——存在主义让位给了实用主义，哲学著作被管理学教材和职场指南取代。这些书的阅读痕迹也不一样了：不再有长篇的旁注，取而代之的是潦草的下划线和偶尔用荧光笔标记的关键词。显然，那几年的我已经没有耐心坐下来慢慢思考，而是急于从书中提取可以立即使用的信息。最上层是最近几年的书，出现了另一个有趣的转折——类型再次回归文学和哲学，但这次不是二十岁时的西方经典，而是东方的禅宗、唐诗和日本美学。这些书几乎没有任何标记和批注，因为我终于学会了一种不试图「抓住」什么的阅读方式——让文字流过去就好，不必非要从中带走一个明确的结论。一个人的书架就是他思想演变的地质剖面图。最底层是热情和困惑的堆积岩，中间是功利和焦虑的沉积层，最上面是一层薄薄的、在无数次断裂和重建之后终于沉淀下来的平静。
text_pinyin: Bān jiā de shíhou, wǒ bùdébù miànduì yī gè jīlěi le shíwǔ nián de shūjià. Qīnglǐ de guòchéng biànchéng le yī cì zìwǒ kǎogǔ——měi yī běn shū de gòumǎi shíjiān hé yuèdú zhuàngtài dōu jīngquè de duìyìng zhe wǒ rénshēng de mǒu gè jiēduàn. Zuì dǐcéng shì dàxué shídài de shū: Sàtè de《Cúnzài yǔ Xūwú》, Jiāmǔ de《Júwàirén》, jǐ běn dú le yībàn de zhéxué rùmén dúwù. Zhèxiē shū de shūyè yǐjīng fànhuáng, kòngbái chù xiě mǎn le wǒ dāngshí zìyǐwéi hěn shēnkè de pīzhù——xiànzài huítóu kàn, nàxiē pīzhù chōngmǎn le yī gè èrshí suì niánqīngrén tèyǒu de rènzhēn hé tiānzhēn. Zhōngjiān jǐ céng shì èrshíwǔ dào sānshí suì zhījiān mǎi de shū, lèixíng fāshēng le míngxiǎn de zhuǎnbiàn——cúnzài zhǔyì ràng wèi gěi le shíyòng zhǔyì, zhéxué zhùzuò bèi guǎnlǐxué jiàocái hé zhíchǎng zhǐnán qǔdài. Zhèxiē shū de yuèdú hénjì yě bù yīyàng le: bú zài yǒu chángpiān de pángzhù, qǔ ér dài zhī de shì liáocǎo de xiàhuáxiàn hé ǒu'ěr yòng yíngguāngbǐ biāojì de guānjiàncí. Xiǎnrán, nà jǐ nián de wǒ yǐjīng méiyǒu nàixīn zuò xiàlái mànmàn sīkǎo, ér shì jí yú cóng shū zhōng tíqǔ kěyǐ lìjí shǐyòng de xìnxī. Zuì shàng céng shì zuìjìn jǐ nián de shū, chūxiàn le lìng yī gè yǒuqù de zhuǎnzhé——lèixíng zàicì huíguī wénxué hé zhéxué, dàn zhè cì bú shì èrshí suì shí de xīfāng jīngdiǎn, ér shì dōngfāng de Chánzōng, Tángshī hé Rìběn měixué. Zhèxiē shū jīhū méiyǒu rènhé biāojì hé pīzhù, yīnwèi wǒ zhōngyú xuéhuì le yī zhǒng bù shìtú「zhuā zhù」shénme de yuèdú fāngshì——ràng wénzì liú guòqù jiù hǎo, bùbì fēi yào cóng zhōng dài zǒu yī gè míngquè de jiélùn. Yī gè rén de shūjià jiù shì tā sīxiǎng yǎnbiàn de dìzhì pōumiàntú. Zuì dǐcéng shì rèqíng hé kùnhuò de duījī yán, zhōngjiān shì gōnglì hé jiāolǜ de chénjī céng, zuì shàngmiàn shì yī céng báo báo de, zài wúshù cì duànliè hé chóngjiàn zhīhòu zhōngyú chéndiàn xiàlái de píngjìng.
text_en: When I moved, I had to face a bookshelf that had accumulated over fifteen years. The clearing-out process became a kind of self-archaeology—the purchase date and reading state of every book corresponded precisely to a phase of my life. The bottom layer held my college-era books: Sartre's Being and Nothingness, Camus's The Stranger, several half-read introductions to philosophy. The pages of these books had yellowed, margins filled with annotations I'd thought very profound at the time—looking back now, those notes are full of the particular earnestness and naivete of a twenty-year-old. The middle tiers held books bought between twenty-five and thirty, with an obvious shift in type—existentialism yielded to pragmatism, philosophical works replaced by management textbooks and workplace guides. The reading traces were different too: no more lengthy marginal notes, replaced by hasty underlines and the occasional keyword highlighted in fluorescent marker. Clearly, the version of me in those years no longer had the patience to sit and think slowly, but was eager to extract immediately usable information from books. The top tier held books from recent years, presenting another interesting turn—the types returned to literature and philosophy, but this time not the Western classics of twenty; instead, Eastern Zen, Tang poetry, and Japanese aesthetics. These books bore almost no marks or annotations, because I had finally learned a way of reading that doesn't try to 'grasp' anything—just let the words flow past; there's no need to carry away a definite conclusion. A person's bookshelf is a geological cross-section of their intellectual evolution. The bottom layer is sedimentary rock of passion and confusion; the middle is a stratum of utilitarianism and anxiety; the very top is a thin layer of calm that finally settled after countless fractures and reconstructions.

title: The Person in the Photograph
text_zh: 整理手机相册的时候，我发现了一张五年前在某个咖啡馆拍的自拍照。照片里的那个人穿着我不记得自己拥有过的衣服，背景是我完全不记得去过的地方，表情介于微笑和发呆之间，像是在想一件已经被彻底遗忘的事情。我盯着这张照片看了很久，感受到一种奇特的陌生感——那个人确实是我，但又不完全是我。五年的时间改变了太多东西：我的脸稍微胖了一点，发际线后退了一厘米左右，眼角多了两条细纹。但这些物理变化是次要的，真正让我感到陌生的是那个表情——五年前的我有一种我现在已经不具备的松弛感，那是一种尚未被某些人生经历打磨过的、带着微量天真的从容。我忽然意识到，照片最残酷的功能不是记录你的样子，而是在未来的某一天让你意识到，你已经回不到那个样子了。每一张照片都是一个时间胶囊，里面封存的不只是你当时的外貌，还有你当时的心境、你当时还不知道即将失去的东西、以及你当时还天真地以为会永远持续的某种状态。我把那张照片放大，想从那个表情里读出更多的信息，但除了那种无法复制的松弛感之外，什么也读不出来。也许这就是照片和记忆的根本区别——记忆是可以被美化和编辑的，而照片是不讲情面的。它不关心你是否准备好了被审视，它只是在那一秒钟忠实地按下了快门，然后把那个你以为转瞬即逝的瞬间，变成了一个永远无法回避的提问：那个人去哪里了？
text_pinyin: Zhěnglǐ shǒujī xiàngcè de shíhou, wǒ fāxiàn le yī zhāng wǔ nián qián zài mǒu gè kāfēiguǎn pāi de zìpāi zhào. Zhàopiàn lǐ de nàge rén chuān zhe wǒ bú jìde zìjǐ yōngyǒu guò de yīfu, bèijǐng shì wǒ wánquán bú jìde qù guò de dìfāng, biǎoqíng jièyú wēixiào hé fādāi zhījiān, xiàng shì zài xiǎng yī jiàn yǐjīng bèi chèdǐ yíwàng de shìqíng. Wǒ dīng zhe zhè zhāng zhàopiàn kàn le hěn jiǔ, gǎnshòu dào yī zhǒng qítè de mòshēnggǎn——nàge rén quèshí shì wǒ, dàn yòu bù wánquán shì wǒ. Wǔ nián de shíjiān gǎibiàn le tài duō dōngxi: wǒ de liǎn shāowēi pàng le yīdiǎn, fàjìxiàn hòutuì le yī límǐ zuǒyòu, yǎnjiǎo duō le liǎng tiáo xì wén. Dàn zhèxiē wùlǐ biànhuà shì cìyào de, zhēnzhèng ràng wǒ gǎn dào mòshēng de shì nàge biǎoqíng——wǔ nián qián de wǒ yǒu yī zhǒng wǒ xiànzài yǐjīng bù jùbèi de sōngchí gǎn, nà shì yī zhǒng shàng wèi bèi mǒuxiē rénshēng jīnglì dǎmó guò de, dài zhe wēiliàng tiānzhēn de cóngróng. Wǒ hūrán yìshí dào, zhàopiàn zuì cánkù de gōngnéng bú shì jìlù nǐ de yàngzi, ér shì zài wèilái de mǒu yī tiān ràng nǐ yìshí dào, nǐ yǐjīng huí bú dào nàge yàngzi le. Měi yī zhāng zhàopiàn dōu shì yī gè shíjiān jiāonáng, lǐmiàn fēngcún de bù zhǐ shì nǐ dāngshí de wàimào, hái yǒu nǐ dāngshí de xīnjìng, nǐ dāngshí hái bù zhīdào jíjiāng shīqù de dōngxi, yǐjí nǐ dāngshí hái tiānzhēn de yǐwéi huì yǒngyuǎn chíxù de mǒu zhǒng zhuàngtài. Wǒ bǎ nà zhāng zhàopiàn fàngdà, xiǎng cóng nàge biǎoqíng lǐ dú chū gèng duō de xìnxī, dàn chúle nà zhǒng wúfǎ fùzhì de sōngchí gǎn zhīwài, shénme yě dú bù chūlái. Yěxǔ zhè jiù shì zhàopiàn hé jìyì de gēnběn qūbié——jìyì shì kěyǐ bèi měihuà hé biānjí de, ér zhàopiàn shì bù jiǎng qíngmiàn de. Tā bù guānxīn nǐ shìfǒu zhǔnbèi hǎo le bèi shěnshì, tā zhǐ shì zài nà yī miǎozhōng zhōngshí de àn xià le kuàimén, ránhòu bǎ nàge nǐ yǐwéi zhuǎnshùn jí shì de shùnjiān, biànchéng le yī gè yǒngyuǎn wúfǎ huíbì de tíwèn: nàge rén qù nǎlǐ le?
text_en: While organizing my phone's photo album, I found a selfie taken in some cafe five years ago. The person in the photo wears clothes I don't remember owning, against a backdrop of a place I have no memory of visiting, with an expression between smiling and daydreaming, as if thinking about something now utterly forgotten. I stared at the photo for a long time and felt an odd sense of estrangement—that person is indeed me, yet not entirely me. Five years changed too many things: my face is slightly fuller, my hairline has retreated about a centimeter, two fine lines have appeared at the corners of my eyes. But these physical changes are secondary; what truly feels foreign is that expression—the me of five years ago possessed a relaxation I no longer have, a composure laced with a trace of naivete that certain life experiences had not yet honed away. I suddenly realized that a photograph's cruelest function is not recording what you look like, but making you realize on some future day that you can never return to that appearance. Every photograph is a time capsule preserving not just your looks at that moment, but your state of mind, the things you didn't yet know you were about to lose, and a certain condition you still naively believed would last forever. I enlarged the photo, trying to read more information from that expression, but beyond that irreproducible ease I could read nothing. Perhaps this is the fundamental difference between photographs and memory—memory can be beautified and edited, while photographs are merciless. They don't care whether you're ready to be scrutinized; they simply press the shutter faithfully at that one second, then transform a moment you thought was fleeting into a question that can never be avoided: where did that person go?
